senses.definition: Relating to Saint Ambrose, his style, or the Christian liturgy connected with him, especially in Milan.
examples: The cathedral still follows the Ambrosian rite in its services.
Scholars compared the hymn to the classic Ambrosian style.
The choir specialized in Ambrosian chants from the early church.

senses.definition: Extremely pleasant, especially in taste or smell, as if fit for the gods.
examples: The dessert had an almost ambrosian sweetness.
An ambrosian aroma filled the kitchen as the bread baked.
They enjoyed an ambrosian meal under the stars.

senses.definition: Plural of ambrosia; different kinds of food, drink, or experiences that taste or feel extremely pleasant.
examples: The menu offered various ambrosias crafted by the chef.
For the exhausted climbers, the simple dishes were ambrosias.
They described the tropical fruits as true ambrosias.

senses.definition: To make something taste or smell very pleasant, like ambrosia; used rarely and mainly in literary style.
examples: The herbs ambrosiated the simple soup.
Sunlight and sea air ambrosiated their modest meal.
The chef sought to ambrosiate every dish on the menu.

senses.definition: A chemical compound derived from the plant ragweed, studied mainly in chemistry and pharmacology.
examples: Researchers examined the structure of ambrosin in the lab.
Ambrosin was isolated from ragweed for further testing.
They published a paper on the biological activity of ambrosin.

senses.definition: A female given name, rare and mainly literary.
examples: Ambrosine waited by the garden gate.
The novel follows Ambrosine through her childhood.
They named their daughter Ambrosine after a character in a poem.

senses.definition: A male given name or family name, mainly in Spanish and Italian-speaking contexts.
examples: Ambrosio grew up in a small coastal town.
Dr. Ambrosio presented the findings at the conference.
They visited the studio of painter Luis Ambrosio.

senses.definition: A steroid alcohol related to cholesterol, studied in organic chemistry and biochemistry.
examples: The team investigated the role of ambrosterol in cell membranes.
Ambrosterol was identified as a minor component in the sample.
They compared ambrosterol with other sterols in the tissue.

senses.definition: A type of early photograph made on a glass plate, popular in the 19th century.
examples: The museum displayed an ambrotype of a Civil War soldier.
She inherited a family ambrotype in a small metal case.
Conservators worked carefully to clean the fragile ambrotype.

senses.definition: Plural of ambrotype; several early photographs made on glass plates.
examples: The archive holds hundreds of Civil War ambrotypes.
They catalogued the ambrotypes for digital preservation.
Collectors value well-preserved ambrotypes highly.

senses.definition: A small cupboard or recess in a church wall used to store sacred vessels or oils.
examples: The priest opened the ambry to take out the holy oils.
The stone ambry was carved into the north wall of the chapel.
Restorers discovered a hidden ambry behind the plaster.

senses.definition: The lowest possible throw of dice, when each die shows one.
examples: He cursed his luck when the dice came up ambsace.
In that game, ambsace meant losing everything.
Superstitious players considered ambsace a bad sign.

senses.definition: Serious bad luck or a very small and worthless amount.
examples: After a day of ambsace, he gave up gambling.
She said his promises were worth ambsace.
The investment brought him ambsace instead of profit.

senses.definition: Plural of ambsace; several throws of ones or several instances of very bad luck or worthlessness.
examples: The night was full of ambsaces and narrow escapes.
He wrote a poem about life's ambsaces and joys.
The gambler remembered the ambsaces that ruined him.

senses.definition: Plural of ambulacrum; the narrow areas on the underside of sea stars and other echinoderms where the tube feet are found.
examples: The ambulacra help the starfish move and hold onto surfaces.
Scientists examined the ambulacra under a microscope.
Damage to the ambulacra affected the sea urchin's movement.

senses.definition: Relating to the radial areas in echinoderms where the tube feet and main body systems run.
examples: The ambulacral grooves are clearly visible on the sea star.
They measured the width of the ambulacral areas.
Ambulacral plates support the tube feet.

senses.definition: Shaped like or resembling an ambulacrum; used in technical descriptions of animals.
examples: The fossil showed an ambulacriform pattern on its surface.
Researchers noted an ambulacriform arrangement of pores.
An ambulacriform structure ran along the body of the organism.

senses.definition: One of the radial areas on the underside of an echinoderm, such as a starfish, where the tube feet are located.
examples: Each ambulacrum contains rows of tube feet.
The starfish lost one ambulacrum in the injury.
They studied the structure of the ambulacrum in detail.

senses.definition: A vehicle used to take sick or injured people to and from hospital.
examples: They called an ambulance after the accident.
The ambulance arrived within ten minutes.
Paramedics helped her into the ambulance.

senses.definition: Taken to or from a place by ambulance; used mainly in medical or news reports.
examples: Two people were ambulanced to the nearest hospital.
He was ambulanced from the stadium after collapsing.
The injured worker was ambulanced in serious condition.

senses.definition: A man who works on an ambulance, especially as a driver or medical assistant.
examples: The ambulanceman reassured the patient during the journey.
An experienced ambulanceman can stay calm in any crisis.
The injured boy thanked the ambulanceman for his help.

senses.definition: Plural of ambulanceman; men who work on ambulances as drivers or medical staff.
examples: The ambulancemen worked through the night after the crash.
Local ambulancemen protested about their long hours.
Two ambulancemen carried the stretcher carefully.

senses.definition: A person who works with or drives an ambulance; less common than 'ambulance driver' or 'paramedic'.
examples: The ambulancer checked the equipment before the shift.
As an ambulancer, she often works nights.
One ambulancer stayed with the patient in the back.

senses.definition: Plural of ambulance; several vehicles used to carry sick or injured people.
examples: Two ambulances arrived at the scene within minutes.
The city bought new ambulances for the hospital.
Ambulances lined up outside the emergency department.

senses.definition: A woman who works on an ambulance, especially as a driver or medical assistant.
examples: The ambulancewoman monitored the patient's breathing.
An experienced ambulancewoman can handle many emergencies.
The injured cyclist thanked the ambulancewoman warmly.

senses.definition: Plural of ambulancewoman; women who work on ambulances as drivers or medical staff.
examples: The ambulancewomen shared stories from their shifts.
Several ambulancewomen were honoured for their bravery.
Two ambulancewomen arrived first at the accident.

senses.definition: The work or activity of operating or working in an ambulance service.
examples: He went into ambulancing after leaving the army.
Ambulancing can be both stressful and rewarding.
They discussed the demands of modern ambulancing.

senses.definition: Able to walk around rather than being confined to a bed or wheelchair, used especially about patients.
examples: The clinic treats both ambulant and non-ambulant patients.
Most patients were ambulant within a few days.
An ambulant patient can go to the bathroom alone.

senses.definition: Moving from place to place, especially in a regular pattern; used mainly in formal or legal styles.
examples: The court sits in several ambulant sessions across the region.
Ambulant traders filled the streets during the festival.
The exhibit will be ambulant, touring different cities.

senses.definition: A street seller who moves from place to place, especially in Spanish- or Portuguese-speaking contexts.
examples: An ambulante sold fruit and snacks outside the station.
The city introduced new rules for ambulantes.
We bought lunch from an ambulante near the beach.

senses.definition: Plural of ambulante; street sellers who move from place to place, especially in Latin American cities.
examples: Ambulantes lined the streets during the festival.
The government met with ambulantes to discuss regulations.
Tourists often buy souvenirs from ambulantes.

senses.definition: People who are able to walk around, especially in a medical context.
examples: The ward was reserved for ambulants needing observation.
Ambulants were allowed to visit the hospital garden.
The clinic treats both ambulants and patients in wheelchairs.

senses.definition: To walk from one place to another, often used in medical descriptions.
examples: The therapist encouraged the patient to ambulate daily.
After surgery, he could ambulate with support.
Patients should ambulate as soon as they feel able.

senses.definition: Walked from place to place, especially as part of medical recovery.
examples: By the third day, she ambulated without help.
The patient ambulated around the ward twice.
He ambulated slowly but steadily after the operation.

senses.definition: Walks from place to place, often used in clinical notes about a patient.
examples: He ambulates independently without a cane.
The child ambulates with minimal assistance.
She ambulates only short distances due to pain.

senses.definition: Walking from place to place, especially during recovery from illness or injury.
examples: The nurse found him ambulating in the corridor.
Ambulating regularly can speed up recovery.
She was ambulating with the help of a frame.

senses.definition: A Latin term meaning walking or a place for walking, used mainly in scholarly or historical writing.
examples: The manuscript refers to an ambulatio around the garden.
In medieval texts, ambulatio often describes covered walkways.
The architect compared the cloister to an ancient ambulatio.

senses.definition: The act of walking or moving from one place to another, especially for exercise or during recovery.
examples: Early ambulation after surgery can prevent complications.
The therapist recorded the patient's ambulation distance.
Ambulation is encouraged as soon as it is safe.

senses.definition: Acts or periods of walking from place to place, often noted in medical or observational records.
examples: Her daily ambulations improved her strength.
The doctor monitored the patient's ambulations on the ward.
His quiet ambulations around the park became a routine.

senses.definition: Relating to or involving walking or moving about, used mainly in technical or formal contexts.
examples: The clinic offers ambulative rehabilitation programs.
Ambulative activities are recommended for heart patients.
They designed an ambulative path through the garden.

senses.definition: A person who walks from place to place, or a device that assists walking; used rarely and technically.
examples: The old city attracted many late-night ambulators.
Engineers tested a new ambulator for stroke patients.
As an ambulator, he preferred walking to any other transport.

senses.definition: Plural form of a technical or Latin term related to places for walking or outpatient clinics, used mainly in specialist writing.
examples: The hospital expanded its ambulatoria to serve more patients.
In older texts, ambulatoria often refers to covered walkways.
Researchers compared several ambulatoria across the region.

senses.definition: Relating to walking or to outpatient medical care where patients do not stay overnight.
examples: The hospital has a new ambulatorial wing for day surgery.
Ambulatorial services reduce the need for beds.
They focused on ambulatorial treatment of chronic diseases.

senses.definition: Plural of ambulatory; covered walkways or aisles around a church or building.
examples: Visitors walked quietly through the dim ambulatories.
The cathedral's ambulatories are decorated with carvings.
Monks used the ambulatories for meditation.

senses.definition: In a way that involves walking or being able to walk, especially in medical descriptions.
examples: The patient is ambulatorily independent after therapy.
Most participants functioned ambulatorily without assistance.
She can manage daily tasks ambulatorily.

senses.definition: An outpatient clinic or department where patients receive treatment without staying overnight.
examples: The ambulatorium specializes in heart disease.
She has regular check-ups at the hospital's ambulatorium.
Funding was provided to build a new ambulatorium.

senses.definition: Plural of ambulatorium; several outpatient clinics or departments.
examples: The city plans to open two new ambulatoriums.
Existing ambulatoriums are struggling with demand.
They compared ambulatoriums in different regions.

senses.definition: People who walk from place to place, or devices that help people walk; used rarely and technically.
examples: Even late at night, a few ambulators could be seen on the streets.
The company tested new ambulators with elderly volunteers.
Therapists adjusted the ambulators for each patient.

senses.definition: Able to walk around; not confined to bed.
examples: Most patients are ambulatory by the third day.
Ambulatory patients may use the hospital garden.
The clinic focuses on ambulatory care.

senses.definition: A covered passage or walkway, especially around the apse of a church.
examples: Tourists walked slowly along the cool ambulatory.
The ambulatory is lined with small chapels.
Monks once used the ambulatory for meditation.

senses.definition: A van or small vehicle used to carry people who need help getting around but are not in an emergency.
examples: The nursing home ordered an ambulette for the appointment.
She travels to dialysis three times a week by ambulette.
The city runs an ambulette service for elderly residents.

senses.definition: Plural of ambulette; several vehicles used to transport patients who are not in emergency situations.
examples: Several ambulettes waited outside the care center.
The hospital replaced its old ambulettes.
Ambulettes provide transport for wheelchair users.

senses.definition: A rare term referring to difficulty in making decisions or a lack of will, used mainly in medical or psychological contexts.
examples: The psychiatrist noted signs of ambulia in the patient.
Ambulia can appear as part of certain brain disorders.
His ambulia made even simple choices hard.

senses.definition: Walking about or moving from place to place, used as a rare or dialect form of 'ambling' or 'ambling around'.
examples: They spent the afternoon ambuling through the old town.
I saw him ambuling along the riverbank.
She enjoyed ambuling slowly after dinner.
